subtopic_detail: Chorionicity determination and surveillance schedule in twin pregnancy
stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 first attends maternity booking at 13+2 weeks gestation by her last menstrual period. Her booking ultrasound demonstrates a viable twin pregnancy. The sonographer identifies a thin inter-twin membrane with a T-sign at the placental insertion. There is a single shared placental disc visible. Both foetuses appear concordant for crown-rump length and nuchal translucency. She has no significant past obstetric history.

data_table.Investigations: Crown-rump length twin 1: 71 mm; twin 2: 73 mm; nuchal translucency twin 1: 1.6 mm, twin 2: 1.7 mm; combined first trimester screen low chance; cervical length 38 mm
data_table.Examination: Booking observations within normal range; uterus consistent with twin pregnancy

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate surveillance plan?

options.text: Continue routine singleton antenatal schedule with ultrasound only at the 20 to 22 week morphology assessment and 36 weeks growth scan
options.rationale: Monochorionic twins require fortnightly ultrasound from 16 weeks for twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome surveillance regardless of low first trimester screen risk. (Source: RANZCOG: Twin Pregnancy)

options.text: Schedule fortnightly ultrasound only after 24 weeks to monitor growth discordance and reserve middle cerebral artery Doppler for symptomatic events
options.rationale: Surveillance starts at 16 weeks for monochorionic twins because twin-to-twin transfusion typically presents between 16 and 26 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Twin Pregnancy)

options.text: Schedule fortnightly ultrasound from 16 weeks for twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome surveillance and plan elective delivery at 36 to 37 weeks
options.rationale: The T-sign indicates monochorionic-diamniotic pregnancy requiring fortnightly surveillance from 16 weeks and delivery at 36 to 37 weeks; later delivery raises stillbirth risk. (Source: RANZCOG: Twin Pregnancy)

options.text: Recommend selective foetal reduction at 14 weeks given the higher pregnancy complication rate in monochorionic-diamniotic twins
options.rationale: Selective reduction is not routine in uncomplicated monochorionic-diamniotic twins; it is reserved for complications such as severe selective growth restriction. (Source: RANZCOG: Twin Pregnancy)

options.text: Plan elective caesarean delivery at 32 weeks to avoid twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome and ensure neonatal intensive care availability
options.rationale: Elective preterm delivery at 32 weeks exposes both twins to significant prematurity morbidity without proportional benefit. (Source: RANZCOG: Twin Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: The T-sign with a single placental disc indicates a monochorionic-diamniotic twin pregnancy. These pregnancies require fortnightly ultrasound from 16 weeks to detect twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome which classically presents between 16 and 26 weeks. Elective delivery at 36+0 to 36+6 weeks balances stillbirth and prematurity risks. Dichorionic twins have a lambda sign and surveillance starts at 20 weeks with growth scans every 4 weeks.
key_points: T-sign means monochorionic; lambda sign means dichorionic
Fortnightly ultrasound from 16 weeks in monochorionic-diamniotic twins
Aim delivery at 36 to 37 weeks for monochorionic-diamniotic, 37 to 38 weeks for dichorionic
explanation.pearls: Chorionicity is most accurate in the first trimester; never defer determination beyond 14 weeks because membranes thin progressively.

stem: A 33-year-old G4P0 attends for preconception counselling. She has had three first trimester miscarriages and one stillbirth at 22 weeks 4 months ago. Investigations after the stillbirth showed persistent lupus anticoagulant positivity on two samples 12 weeks apart and elevated anticardiolipin IgG. She has no personal venous thromboembolism history. Her partner is supportive and she would like to conceive again.

data_table.Examination: BMI 24, BP 118/76, no skin lesions, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: Persistent lupus anticoagulant on two occasions 12 weeks apart; anticardiolipin IgG 60 GPL; beta-2 glycoprotein-I 45 SGU; antinuclear antibody and double-stranded DNA negative; FBC, renal and liver function normal

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate pharmacological plan once pregnancy is confirmed?

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily alone from positive pregnancy test, continuing throughout pregnancy and the puerperium without anticoagulation
options.rationale: Obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome with prior late pregnancy loss requires both low-dose aspirin and prophylactic low-molecular-weight heparin. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily plus prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg daily from confirmation of pregnancy through to 6 weeks postpartum
options.rationale: Obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome is treated with combined low-dose aspirin and prophylactic low-molecular-weight heparin antenatally and for 6 weeks postpartum. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)

options.text: Warfarin titrated to an INR of 2 to 3 from the second trimester onwards with bridging enoxaparin at delivery and post-partum
options.rationale: Warfarin crosses the placenta and is teratogenic; it is not used antenatally for obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome. (Source: AMH: Anticoagulants in Pregnancy)

options.text: Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg twice daily alone in lieu of anticoagulation throughout pregnancy and postpartum
options.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine is adjunctive in lupus-associated antiphospholipid syndrome but not a substitute for aspirin and low-molecular-weight heparin. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)

options.text: Therapeutic enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg daily plus prednisolone 10 mg daily from the first positive pregnancy test
options.rationale: Therapeutic anticoagulation is reserved for those with prior venous thromboembolism; prednisolone is not first-line for obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)

explanation.summary: Obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome is defined by persistent antiphospholipid antibody positivity 12 weeks apart together with pregnancy morbidity such as three or more consecutive first trimester losses or fetal death after 10 weeks. Management is combined low-dose aspirin plus prophylactic enoxaparin from confirmation of pregnancy through to 6 weeks postpartum to reduce venous thromboembolism and improve live-birth rates. Therapeutic anticoagulation is reserved for prior venous thromboembolism. Hydroxychloroquine and prednisolone are adjunctive in lupus-associated cases.
key_points: Aspirin plus prophylactic enoxaparin for obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome
Therapeutic dosing reserved for prior venous thromboembolism
explanation.pearls: Confirm antibody persistence at least 12 weeks apart before labelling antiphospholipid syndrome; transient positivity post-infection is common.

subtopic_detail: Severe aortic stenosis with limited exercise capacity in pregnancy planning
stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 attends for preconception counselling. She has bicuspid aortic valve disease with a peak gradient of 75 mmHg and mean gradient of 52 mmHg on echocardiography last month. The aortic valve area is 0.9 cm2. She walks 600 m on flat ground before stopping for dyspnoea and has had one syncopal episode while running. Left ventricular ejection fraction is 60%. Her cardiologist's letter notes no aortopathy. She has no other comorbidity.

data_table.Examination: Ejection systolic murmur grade 3 over 6 at the right upper sternal edge radiating to carotids; soft second heart sound; no displaced apex; no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: Echocardiogram: peak gradient 75 mmHg, mean gradient 52 mmHg, valve area 0.9 cm2, no left ventricular hypertrophy, ascending aortic diameter 38 mm; ECG sinus rhythm with normal axis

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate counselling and management plan now?

options.text: Counsel that her cardiac lesion is modified World Health Organization class II and proceed with planned conception now with shared-care obstetric review
options.rationale: Severe symptomatic aortic stenosis with prior syncope is modified World Health Organization class IV with extreme maternal morbidity and mortality risk. (Source: ESC: Cardiovascular Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: Recommend pregnancy can proceed but with combined cardiology-obstetric monthly review and elective caesarean delivery at 39 weeks under regional anaesthesia
options.rationale: Recommending conception in severe symptomatic aortic stenosis without first addressing the valve violates contemporary preconception care standards. (Source: ESC: Cardiovascular Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: Advise the use of oestrogen-containing combined oral contraception to avoid pregnancy while continuing routine annual cardiology follow-up appointments
options.rationale: Oestrogen-containing contraception is contraindicated due to thrombotic risk in severe valvular heart disease; progestogen-only methods are preferred. (Source: RANZCOG: Contraception)

options.text: Counsel that the lesion is modified World Health Organization class IV, advise pregnancy avoidance, and refer for valve intervention with reassessment afterwards
options.rationale: Severe symptomatic aortic stenosis with syncope is a modified World Health Organization class IV lesion; pregnancy is contraindicated until valve intervention reduces gradient and symptoms. (Source: ESC: Cardiovascular Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: Plan immediate valve replacement using a mechanical prosthesis followed by warfarin throughout future pregnancies for thromboprophylaxis
options.rationale: Valve choice in young women must consider warfarin teratogenicity and pregnancy plans; bioprosthetic or transcatheter options are often discussed. (Source: ESC: Cardiovascular Disease in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: The modified World Health Organization classification stratifies maternal cardiovascular risk. Severe symptomatic aortic stenosis with syncope or limited exercise tolerance is class IV with maternal mortality of up to 10%. Pregnancy is contraindicated and women should be advised effective contraception, ideally progestogen-only, while valve intervention is planned. Reassessment of class after valve intervention guides safe future conception. Mechanical valves complicate pregnancy because warfarin is teratogenic and dose-dependent, while bioprostheses degrade faster but allow heparin-based regimens.
key_points: Severe symptomatic aortic stenosis with syncope is modified World Health Organization class IV
Pregnancy is contraindicated until valve gradient and symptoms are reduced
Avoid oestrogen-containing contraception in significant valvular heart disease
explanation.pearls: Mean gradient over 50 mmHg, valve area under 1.0 cm2, or symptoms define severe aortic stenosis; symptomatic disease in young women always merits valve intervention discussion before pregnancy.

subtopic_detail: Schizoaffective disorder vs bipolar I with psychotic features
stem: A 24-year-old woman is admitted with a third episode of acute psychosis with prominent persecutory delusions, third-person auditory hallucinations, and disorganised behaviour. Collateral from her sister indicates these psychotic symptoms have been present for the past 6 weeks. During the first 3 weeks of this episode she was sleeping 3 hours nightly, spending compulsively, and grandiose. Over the past 3 weeks her mood has stabilised but the psychotic symptoms persist unchanged.

data_table.Examination: Mental state: thought disorder, persecutory delusions held with full conviction, third-person auditory hallucinations; mood euthymic for the past 3 weeks; no current suicidal ideation
data_table.Investigations: Urine drug screen negative; CT brain normal; TSH, B12, FBC, calcium, electrolytes normal; structured interview confirms two prior episodes of mania each lasting at least 7 days

lead_in: What is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder bipolar type because the psychosis has persisted at least 2 weeks in the absence of prominent mood symptoms during a longitudinal course with mood episodes
options.rationale: Schizoaffective disorder requires psychosis without prominent mood symptoms for at least 2 weeks alongside mood episodes meeting full criteria over the illness course. (Source: RANZCP: Schizophrenia Clinical Practice Guideline)

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with mood-congruent psychotic features because mania was clearly present in the first 3 weeks of this index episode of illness
options.rationale: Bipolar I with psychotic features requires psychosis to occur only within mood episodes; persistence of psychosis after mood remits points to schizoaffective. (Source: RANZCP: Schizophrenia Clinical Practice Guideline)

options.text: Schizophreniform disorder because the psychotic symptoms have been present for over 1 month but less than 6 months in total since the prodromal onset
options.rationale: Schizophreniform disorder does not include episodes meeting full mania or major depression criteria during the course of psychosis. (Source: RANZCP: Schizophrenia Clinical Practice Guideline)

options.text: Substance-induced psychotic disorder because methamphetamine remains detectable for up to 3 months in clinical practice despite a current negative screen
options.rationale: Methamphetamine is undetectable in urine after 72 to 96 hours; substance-induced psychosis would also be expected to remit fully within weeks of cessation. (Source: RANZCP: Psychostimulant Position Statement)

options.text: Major depressive disorder with psychotic features and mood incongruent persecutory delusions despite the absence of depressive symptoms over recent weeks
options.rationale: Major depression with psychotic features requires the psychosis to occur during a current depressive episode, which this presentation lacks. (Source: RANZCP: Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCP: Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guideline

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder requires psychosis to persist for at least 2 weeks in the absence of prominent mood symptoms within a course that also includes mood episodes meeting major depressive or manic criteria for a substantial part of the illness. Bipolar disorder with psychotic features confines psychotic symptoms within the duration of mood episodes. Treatment for schizoaffective bipolar type usually combines a mood stabiliser with an antipsychotic; clozapine is considered for treatment resistance. The diagnosis often requires longitudinal data because of significant overlap with bipolar disorder with psychotic features.
key_points: Schizoaffective requires 2 weeks of psychosis without prominent mood symptoms
Mood episodes must be present for a substantial part of the illness
Treatment combines mood stabiliser plus antipsychotic; consider clozapine for resistance
explanation.pearls: Longitudinal collateral history outperforms cross-sectional assessment for distinguishing schizoaffective from bipolar with psychotic features.

sources.label: RANZCP: Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guideline

stem: A 47-year-old smokes 25 cigarettes daily for 28 years and presents for cessation support after a hospital admission for a non-ST elevation myocardial infarction 4 weeks ago. He has previously tried nicotine patches and tried cold turkey twice without success. He is on aspirin, atorvastatin, metoprolol, perindopril. His mood is euthymic with no previous depression or suicidal thoughts. He has no renal impairment.

data_table.Examination: BMI 28, BP 132/82, no signs of withdrawal, no respiratory disease on examination
data_table.Investigations: eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73 m2, normal liver function, recent echocardiogram with preserved ejection fraction

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate plan for varenicline?

options.text: Start varenicline 1 mg twice daily today and stop smoking immediately on the same day as the first dose to maximise medication coverage at the receptor
options.rationale: Varenicline requires a 1 week titration from 0.5 mg daily to 1 mg twice daily and the quit date is set within that first or second week, not immediately at first dose. (Source: AMH: Varenicline)

options.text: Substitute varenicline 1 mg twice daily for his metoprolol because beta-blockers interfere with the action of varenicline at nicotinic receptors
options.rationale: Beta-blockers do not interact pharmacologically with varenicline; both are continued and metoprolol is essential after recent myocardial infarction. (Source: AMH: Varenicline)

options.text: Avoid varenicline entirely after recent acute coronary syndrome and recommend nicotine inhalator plus oral nortriptyline combination instead
options.rationale: Recent meta-analyses show no excess cardiovascular events with varenicline; it remains a first-line option after acute coronary syndrome. (Source: RACGP: Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Begin varenicline 2 mg daily with a planned cessation date 6 weeks after starting to allow gradual tapering of cigarettes during titration
options.rationale: The standard quit date is set 1 to 2 weeks after starting, not 6 weeks; the dose of 2 mg daily is also non-standard. (Source: AMH: Varenicline)

options.text: Start varenicline 0.5 mg daily for 3 days then 0.5 mg twice daily for 4 days then 1 mg twice daily, with a quit date 8 to 14 days from first dose, for 12 weeks
options.rationale: Standard titration is 0.5 mg daily for 3 days, 0.5 mg twice daily for 4 days, then 1 mg twice daily; the quit date is set 8 to 14 days after starting for 12 weeks. (Source: AMH: Varenicline)

explanation.summary: Varenicline is a partial nicotinic agonist that is started 1 to 2 weeks before the planned quit date with a standard titration to allow tolerance. Standard duration is 12 weeks; an additional 12 weeks may be considered to consolidate abstinence. Australian post-acute coronary syndrome guidance still considers varenicline first-line because cardiovascular safety is established. Common adverse effects include nausea, vivid dreams, and insomnia; severe psychiatric adverse effects are rare and historic boxed warnings have been substantially revised.
key_points: Set the quit date 1 to 2 weeks after starting varenicline
Titrate over 7 days then continue 1 mg twice daily for 12 weeks
Cardiovascular safety after acute coronary syndrome is established
explanation.pearls: Nausea peaks in the first 2 weeks of varenicline; taking the dose with food and a full glass of water reduces it markedly.
